lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market employer firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced local courier, scheduled route, same-day, medical, legal, retail, grocery, meal, and small-parcel delivery services to external customers, where commercial accounts, dispatch capability, and operations can transfer with control.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure software marketplaces without controlled delivery operations, individual gig couriers and owner-only messenger work, captive merchant fleets, internal delivery units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Per-delivery, per-stop, route-day, mileage, subscription, or negotiated account fees paid by merchants, institutions, platforms, or end consumers; repeat revenue ranges from contracted dedicated routes to on-demand orders.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 492210 mixes employer-based courier and dedicated-route operators, individual gig work, and software marketplace models whose labor, transferability, and commercial economics are not coherent in one screen. The lens retains independent employer-based delivery operators with transferable external accounts and excludes pure platforms and solo gig couriers; this is a business-model coherence boundary, not an attractiveness selection.

a.rationale: BLS's 2022 four-digit industry table shows 77.06% of employment in transportation and material-moving occupations, including 58.01% light-truck drivers, while office and administrative support was 17.09%, including 2.10% dispatchers and 7.89% couriers and messengers. AI can automate or compress order intake, dispatch, routing, ETA communication, customer service, proof-of-delivery processing, billing, scheduling, and exception triage, and can assist some delivery task time. It cannot readily substitute most driving, pickup, handoff, loading, and irregular-site work within five years. The wage weighting of supervisors, managers, and office roles lifts opportunity above a simple clerical headcount share.
a.change_evidence: A current six-digit wage-and-staffing extract joined to task-level time studies from independent local-delivery employers, separating driving, waiting, pickup/handoff, dispatch, service contacts, route planning, claims, billing, and proof-of-delivery work.
caveats: The available OEWS table is four-digit NAICS 492200 rather than six-digit 492210 and excludes self-employed workers.
The 2022 occupation mix may not reflect subsequent platform growth or automation.
The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and an IPS harmonization, whose vintage and basis uncertainty are separate from the task-share estimate.

rho.rationale: Dispatch, route sequencing, customer communications, documentation, and billing are feasible with current tools. DoorDash reports an AI dispatcher that matches orders to delivery methods in real time, demonstrating operational feasibility at platform scale. LMM operators face poorer data, fragmented customer integrations, driver adoption, privacy and cybersecurity requirements, safety constraints, and limited capital. Autonomous delivery remains geographically narrow and complements human delivery rather than making it the base implementation case.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after cohorts from independent regional delivery operators showing implementation dates, dispatch headcount, deliveries per route-hour, empty miles, late-delivery and failed-handoff rates, service contacts, claims, churn, and capital/software costs over 24 months.
caveats: DoorDash has scale, data, and capital unavailable to typical LMM operators, so its deployment overstates small-firm implementation capacity.
The included subsegments differ in urgency, chain-of-custody rules, delivery density, and tolerance for automated handoff.
Implementation excludes pricing and demand effects.

e.rationale: Within any true LMM employer-firm population, eligible candidates must control recurring external accounts and a delivery operation that survives the owner. The code also contains solo couriers, platform-directed labor, captive-like fleets, highly concentrated subcontractors, and businesses whose value is chiefly the owner's route relationships. Those exclusions materially reduce eligibility. The frozen margin-bridged dataset reports zero firms in the EBITDA band, so this share is conditional on such firms existing and does not override the dataset input.
e.change_evidence: A verified census from Dun & Bradstreet, Grata, Experian, state motor-carrier records, and local courier associations, with revenue, normalized EBITDA, employee status, customer contracts, platform dependence, owner labor, fleet control, and account concentration for every candidate.
caveats: No source directly measures eligibility for the narrowed lens.

The frozen n equals zero and is an estimate derived from SUSB size classes and a broad transportation margin; e is therefore conditional and has no practical population unless diligence finds band-qualified firms.
Classification boundaries between a delivery operator, marketplace, staffing supplier, and captive fleet are porous.

s5.bridge: Gallup surveyed 1,264 US owners across industries in fall 2024, not local-delivery owners: 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years, compared with 14% across all owners primarily working in their businesses. I reduce the center to 18% because planned gifts and internal transfers may not qualify, and owner dependence, platform contracts, customer concentration, and the dataset's zero estimated LMM count can prevent a local-delivery operation from completing a qualifying control transfer. Consolidation or retirement can raise the realized rate; closures and failed processes lower it.
s5.rationale: The broad employer-owner survey supplies the correct five-year timing but not industry completion data. The narrowed lens improves transferability relative to solo gig work, yet contracts, drivers, operating procedures, insurance, and customer relationships must persist through a sale.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year completed and failed sale records from BizBuySell, Axial, PitchBook, GF Data, SBA 7(a) loans, and local courier brokers tagged to 492210, including gifts, internal successions, closures, listings, withdrawals, and buyer type.
caveats: The proxy is cross-industry owner intention, not completed qualifying control transfers.
Gallup's category includes gifts and rare public offerings, and respondents may own firms outside the LMM band.
Because the frozen n is zero, this probability is conditional on an eligible firm population that the dataset does not identify.

q.rationale: Fixed route-day and per-delivery pricing can let an operator initially retain route-density, dispatch, and administrative savings. Retention erodes through rebids, merchant negotiations, platform rate changes, customer switching, and price competition. DoorDash characterizes local food delivery as fragmented and intensely competitive and states that competitive pressure can force fee, commission, incentive, and pricing changes. Specialized chain-of-custody or service-level contracts support the upper end; commodity on-demand work supports the lower end.
q.change_evidence: Contract and invoice cohorts for independent delivery firms showing rate basis, renewal and rebid cadence, escalation clauses, platform rate-setting, merchant concentration, gross margin, churn, service credits, and realized price for three years after efficiency changes.
caveats: DoorDash is a marketplace-scale comparator rather than an LMM contract courier.
Medical and legal routes may have stronger service differentiation than restaurant or retail delivery.
The estimate excludes implementation failure and demand loss, which belong in rho and d5/o.

d5.bridge: BLS projected four-digit NAICS 492200 employment from 189,000 in 2023 to 214,300 in 2033, a 13.4% ten-year increase. A roughly half-horizon translation implies about 6% over five years. Employment is not constant-price, constant-quality service demand, and the four-digit category is broader than 492210; productivity may make employment growth understate quantity, while mix shifts toward platform or self-employed delivery and quality changes may make it overstate demand for the narrowed employer-operator basket.
d5.rationale: Continued consumer use of local commerce delivery and expansion into grocery and retail support quantity, while in-store pickup, macroeconomic sensitivity, merchant self-delivery, platform consolidation, and substitution away from employer-based local couriers cap growth. DoorDash's 2025 total orders grew 23%, but that global platform growth is too broad and fast-changing to use directly as the current-basket forecast.
d5.change_evidence: US constant-price delivery counts from local-delivery associations, platform disclosures, merchant cohorts, and operator route data, split by restaurant, grocery, retail, medical, legal, parcel, platform, merchant-owned fleet, and independent employer operator through 2031.
caveats: The BLS projection has a ten-year horizon, is at four-digit NAICS, and measures employment rather than service quantity.
DoorDash's reported orders span more than 40 countries and multiple marketplace and commerce products, not the narrowed US operator lens.
Real demand is sensitive to delivery fees, consumer spending, merchant economics, and regulation.

o.rationale: The current basket still requires physical pickup, custody, transport, and handoff, and DoorDash says human Dashers will continue to complete the vast majority of its daily deliveries even as it deploys autonomous methods. However, the operator of the narrowed kind faces genuine displacement by merchant-owned fleets, dominant marketplaces, platform-directed independent couriers, pickup, lockers, drones, sidewalk robots, and small autonomous vehicles. DoorDash launched commercial early access for its Dot robot in two Arizona cities and uses AI to choose among humans, robots, and drones, showing that nontraditional supply is no longer purely hypothetical.
o.change_evidence: Metro-level order shares by human employee courier, gig courier, merchant fleet, pickup/locker, drone, sidewalk robot, and road robot, plus merchant procurement plans and regulatory approvals across the largest US delivery markets through 2031.
caveats: Commercial autonomous deployment cited is an early program in Tempe and Mesa and may not scale broadly within five years.
Human gig couriers still count as operator-required delivery in a physical sense but may displace firms under the narrowed employer-operator lens.
Specialized medical and legal deliveries may retain human accountability longer than meals or small retail items.

narrative.executive_view: Local delivery has a high labor ratio and concrete AI opportunities in dispatch, routing, service, proof of delivery, and administration, but the firm opportunity is structurally uncertain: the frozen dataset estimates zero firms in the target EBITDA band, and the NAICS code mixes transferable employer operators with gig workers and software platforms. Any thesis must begin by proving that a qualifying firm population exists.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can automate order ingestion, dispatch assignment, route sequencing, ETA messages, customer support, billing, claims, and exception triage. It can also coordinate mixed fleets and, in limited geographies, autonomous vehicles. Most local-delivery work remains embodied in driving, pickup, waiting, custody, and doorstep handoff, so the near-term benefit is greater throughput and avoided dispatch/administrative hiring rather than wholesale removal of couriers.
narrative.value_capture: Contract routes and specialized service levels can retain efficiency gains until renewal, while commodity on-demand work faces rapid price comparison and platform-set economics. Fragmented competition, merchant bargaining, promotions, driver incentives, and regulation make five-year retention uncertain and segment-dependent.
narrative.firm_availability: The cross-industry employer-owner survey implies meaningful transfer intent, but there is no direct evidence of a band-qualified 492210 population, consistent with the frozen n of zero. A transferable target needs customer contracts, operating procedures, dispatch systems, insurance, and a driver base that survive the owner; solo routes and platform-dependent contractors generally fail that test.
narrative.demand_durability: BLS projects continued employment growth for local messengers and local delivery, and platform order volumes show strong consumer use. Yet growth in physical local delivery does not guarantee demand for independent employer operators: merchant fleets, large platforms, gig networks, pickup, and autonomous methods can capture the same service quantity.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest risk is population validity, followed by business-model heterogeneity, worker-classification and pay regulation, insurance and safety, cyber dependence, account concentration, price sensitivity, and autonomous or platform substitution. The frozen labor ratio mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and the zero firm count is margin-bridged from broad transportation economics; both require careful interpretation without re-estimation.
narrative.principal_driver: High labor intensity and repeat local-delivery workflows create practical gains from AI-assisted dispatch, routing, service, and administration.
narrative.principal_weakness: The frozen dataset identifies no firms in the target EBITDA band, while platforms, gig labor, merchant fleets, and autonomy challenge the durability of an independent employer-operator population.

sources.title: NAICS 492200 - Local Messengers and Local Delivery: May 2022 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Four-digit occupation mix: 180,680 total employment; office and administrative support 17.09%; transportation and material moving 77.06%; light-truck drivers 58.01%; dispatchers 2.10%; estimates exclude self-employed workers.

sources.title: DoorDash, Inc. Annual Report for year ended December 31, 2025
sources.supports: Describes transaction, delivery, and merchant fee models; independent courier pay; intense and fragmented local-delivery competition; merchant-owned fleets; pricing sensitivity; more than 40-country scope; and 2025 total orders of 3.172 billion, up 23%.

sources.title: DoorDash Unveils Dot, the Delivery Robot Powered by its Autonomous Delivery Platform
sources.supports: Documents September 2025 commercial early access in Tempe and Mesa, an AI dispatcher selecting among human, robot, and drone delivery, and DoorDash's statement that Dashers will continue to complete the vast majority of daily deliveries.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall 2024 survey of 1,264 US owners: 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, versus 14% among all owners primarily working in their business.

sources.title: Industry and occupational employment projections overview and highlights, 2023-33
sources.supports: BLS projection for NAICS 492200 from 189,000 jobs in 2023 to 214,300 in 2033, a 13.4% increase.
